Painting: this work illustrates an allegory by the medieval author Sebastian Brant
Painting: a skeleton holding a silver arrow peers through a door at a bedridden man dropping coins into a chest
Painting: a dying man ignores a small crucifix in a window to reach for a bag of coins offered by a demon
Painting: this work is exhibited opposite The Ship of Fools at the National Gallery of Art
Painting: multiple versions of this triptych exist, all placing Jesus at the top of the central panel within a heavenly blue sphere with trumpeting angels
Painting: a pink-heavy version of this triptych in Bruges features a pink-clad Christ and souls ferried by a pink boat in Eden
Painting: the Vienna version of this triptych shows a glutton forced to drink from an enormous cauldron

Artist: frequently cited as a proto-Surrealist alongside Arcimboldo
Artist: the burning of this painter's hometown may have inspired the blackened buildings recurring in his works
Artist: works primarily housed in the Prado in Madrid
Artist: Hieronymus Cock initially credited Bruegel's Big Fish Eat Little Fish drawing to this painter to boost sales
Artist: Czesław Miłosz wrote an ekphrastic poem about this painter's most famous triptych, asserting "it's possible to conjecture that mankind exists / To provision and populate Hell"
Artist: Henry Miller titled a memoir Big Sur and the Oranges of this painter
